context:
It lays claim to being the most isolated human settlement on earth, a volcanic archipelago in the South Atlantic Ocean, home to just under 270 people. Now, the tiny settlement of Tristan da Cunha is seeking the help of architects and designers the world over to secure its future well into the 21st century. With the help of the Royal Institute of British Architects (RIBA), the UK overseas territory is holding an international competition to improve aging infrastructure and make the island self-sustainable as it approaches the 200th anniversary of its settlement. As a sign of just how removed Tristan da Cunha is from the rest of the world, there are only seven family names on the islands. Those who live there are mainly the descendants of Dutch, American and Italian sailors as well as 19th century British explorers. There are also a handful of expatriate Brits. But watching hit TV series "Lost"or reading up on William Golding's "Lord of the Flies" won't be required research for competition entrants. Instead, those with experience of renewable energy in remote regions, modern housing and developing cost-effective agrarian systems are encouraged to apply. "The competition is looking at enhancing the built environment on the island (and) making all structures more energy efficient," the island's administrator, Alex Mitcham told CNN when visiting the UK recently. Mitcham explained that most existing homes and public buildings on Tristan da Cunha are small, bungalow-type structures made from made from a mix of concrete and local volcanic rock. Few have simple insulation that can lead to energy savings while the infrastructure for things like renewable power generation and internet provision remain limited. The island is also only accessible by sea 60-days-a-year through a small port in its only town, Edinburgh of the Seven Seas. According to James Porter of RIBA Competitions, contestants will have to factor in irregular design questions such as "how can we get the material ashore and how can we actually think of ways that we can put things together once there?" "There are limitations on the amount of cargo that can be trans-shipped," he added. In total, Tristan da Cunha is made up of four islands. Tristan is the largest land mass and the only one to house people. Two more unpopulated islands, Nightingale and Inaccessible, lie close by. A third, Gough, is situated roughly 230 miles away. Both Gough and Inaccessible are UNESCO World Heritage sites. The closest mainland port is Cape Town, South Africa, some seven days sailing time and 1,750 miles north-east. Tristan already has a pub, shop, cafe, dance hall, museum, catholic church and swimming pool. There are also two expatriate doctors, two Tristanian nurses and school teachers with some 30 students of varying ages. The island's main industry is lobster fishing, with exports reaching as far afield as the U.S., Australia, Japan and the EU thanks to a fishing company that has exclusive rights to fish in the surrounding waters. Tristan earns additional income from the sale of unique stamps, coins and handicraft souvenirs, Mitcham explained. Modest revenue also flows from tourism and visitor accommodation. "As it is the remotest inhabited island in the world, the Tristanians are already very self sufficient, being able to produce many of their own foodstuffs (like fish, livestock and potatoes)," Mitcham said. However, the cost of living has increased in recent years, he added. With limited transport and communication links, the islanders are also keen to see how they can reduce their reliance on fossil fuels, improve the energy efficiency of properties and reduce imports. "We are slowly updating 40-year old infrastructure," Mitcham said of the last time the island experienced a major redesign. That modernization happened roughly a decade after the island was evacuated temporarily due to ongoing volcanic activity. Before that period, the main way for the islanders to communicate with the outside world was by Morse code. Things have already changed a lot since then, however. "A new water system, electrical system and waste water system have just been completed," Mitcham said. "And with the 200th Anniversary of the founding of Tristan (2016) almost upon us it is an appropriate time to look at new initiatives that may be able to help the future viability of the community." Interested architects have until mid-June to submit their initial ideas. The best will be whittled down to an initial 20 and then five after further analysis between June and September. Porter and Mitcham say the islanders will have to apply to the UK and possibly the EU for grants to fund the project. The winner is penciled in to be announced in June 2016. The most important aspect for applicants is to "identify cost-effective and practical solutions to improve the energy efficiency (through renewable energy sources) of properties," Mitcham said "as well as modernizing ... government structures."

Without exaggerating... the chicken fingers come at 2:55p.  Then the nachos a couple minutes later.  Then later, the bread bowl.  (We asked for some place settings.) Then finally the fish tacos around 3:05p.   

40-50 minutes after ordering is ridiculous.

To backtrack.  The waiter was amazing, super nice guy.  He kept apologizing for the delay in food, which other tables were experiencing.  I felt bad for him because it's not his fault but the restaurant needs to figure this out.  I know it's hard with Covid and people calling in sick but they can't keep 'every' customer waiting over 40 minutes for bar food.  The waiter took off the nachos from the bill, to make-up for it, so that was nice.  

If it wasn't for the nice weather and great views, we would have left, honestly.  

Now... the food.  It was all really good.  The clam chowder was amazing, really loved it.  The chicken fingers were perfect.  The fish tacos were deep-fried fish, a little too oily, but still good.  I would probably get the popcorn shrimp tacos next time.  The only dud was the nachos; the chicken looked like it was boiled chicken, the chips were soggy, and the cheese was half-melted... clearly microwaved.

During the order I asked if it was OK to use the buy one get one free deal from the Axxess card and they said yes.

So we wait, and when they ring me up the total is over $22. I was confused and asked about the total. This is the point at which he informed me that I have to pay for the smoothie separately ($4.75) as the buy 1 get 1 will only apply to the food.

I was pretty shocked that he waited until the last moment to charge me like this. He clearly wrote just two items on the order, the plate and the combo, it is clearly labeled as a combo item, but he looked at me and said "well, what do you want to do now?".

I said I didn't want to pay extra since I told him I was using the buy one get one deal, he wrote down two items, and said nothing about this until now. He said "It's still a good deal" but I was so annoyed I explained why I was frustrated. He took the smoothie and put it under the counter and rang me up for $15.75.

I am so surprised that a $4.75 smoothie that almost certainly was going to be thrown away was more important to them than a happy customer who was just trying to use the promotion that they were running. As I left I asked one more time "Are you really sure I can't just have the smoothie?" He said no, I can't have it and I walked out feeling pretty stressed about the whole thing.

I could have ordered two tortas or two mole's that cost the same if I knew they were going to do this. I am just so surprised by the greed. I totally understand if he told me this when I was ordering and made it clear I was using the buy one get one deal. No complaints. But to wait until the end, try to get an extra $4.75, then take the smoothie and act like I am being the jerk just blows me away.

I won't ever go back and I'll tell this story every time this place comes up in conversation in town. I hope it was worth the $4.75.

I have since found out that the person who rang me up and made all these choices was in fact the owner. We found the seating to be comfortable and the place not overly crowded nor overly noisy on a Friday night. The bartender (I wish I knew his name) is one heck of a nice guy! So friendly and efficient. The House Chardonnay ($5/glass during HH) was delicious and the food was good (not super great but good) and served promptly. We were told they'd be happy to provide table service to us even if we located ourselves up on the landing/seating area just outside of the bar area (between the bar and the lobby). I was so impressed with the bartender's and the server's relaxed, easy-going, and yet eager-to-please approach. The HH extends to 7pm which is generous. Besides House Red and House White wine during HH, they also make available a beer on tap for $5 and a list of 5 or so appetizers at a reduced price including chips with gauc and salsa, egg rolls with chili sauce, or hummus dip with bread.

The Barra los Arcos outside patio is awesome as well. There is a variety of seating options there including chairs around tables, chairs around fire pits, and even two patio swings big enough for three people (although probably meant for two). The table we were given was adjacent and protruding into the passage the waiters used to get and deliver the food. This table was also on a line to the scullery. As a result all through the meal waiters and busboys were quickly passing right by our table on the way to or from the kitchen and scullery. The constant back and forth was annoying and noisy. The noise from the dishes being placed in the scullery was extremely noisy and piercing to the ear. In addition it took three requests to get cream for Lynn's tea. The tea had cooled down considerably by the time the cream arrived. A design flaw placed the buffet foods adjacent to the outside row of dining tables. Those diners had people looking down at them as they gathered their food. For us the people gathering their food blocked our view to the outside courtyard. The waiters seemed overtaxed. Our waiter said they were not understaffed but overloaded with dinners. Seems at a quality restaurant there would be ample personnel to meet the needs of the diners. Lastly my ham was overcooked, dry and tough, the eggs were not prepared as requested, potatoes were so so, and the toast was hard. However, my wife's lox and bagel were wonderful. I reported all of the above in addition to the suggestion that they remove our table and go for quality rather than quantity to the concierge as there was no one else to report it to.

When I visited last, Marina took our order of Grilled Mahi taco, Pastor taco, Sea bass taco and a Cucumber lime drink.  She was very outgoing and friendly.  
All salsas are full of flavor and delicious, we couldn't decide which was our favorite!  Sad to see this place gone...  :("}, {'review_stars': 4.0, 'review_date': '2015-12-07 05:27:39', 'review_text': 'The tacos here are very good, although a bit on the pricey side.  The main problem with this place is that they aren't reliably open when they say they will be.

I've been here three or four times now.  They have a lineup of seemingly ordinary taco styles like carnitas, barbacoa, and al pastor, but the execution is anything but ordinary.  The al pastor and barbacoa are unusually succulent and delicious.  On top of these, they have fish tacos that set a high bar for other taquerias to meet.  They also set a high bar for your wallet to meet, though: their signature "mango pescado" taco will set you back $6.  (Admittedly, it's a bit larger than an ordinary taco, and according to the guy they use locally-caught fish, which is cool.)  The salsa lineup doesn't disappoint either.

The place is tiny, so seating is ultra-minimal: a small counter inside and two tiny tables just outside the door.  You eat off plates displaying the likeness of Jesus Christ, the virgin Mary, or the like.  Cash only.

This would be a go-to place in my taco rotation but for one fatal flaw: they don't keep reliable hours.  A sign on the door purports to show the hours that they're open, but they don't keep to that schedule.  I came by with a friend at about 8:05pm one night.  The sign said they were open until 9pm that night, but the place was totally closed --- not like just cleaning up, but totally dark and clearly had closed up much earlier.  Another time, when it was open, I asked the guy behind the counter what their hours were, and he couldn't give a straight answer either,.  Yet another time, while waiting for my tacos, I saw an employee of SBMenus (a local restaurant delivery service) come in and tell the Tacos El Rey employee that SBMenus has had to cancel delivery orders because the place wasn't open.  I've also popped by now and then when walking around downtown, only to find it closed probably about half the time.

To me, not being open when you say you'll be is a major transgression; I considered knocking them down to three stars but decided to be kind and stick with 4.  It's also doubly peculiar because the location is half a block off of lower State, a stone's throw from various bars and tons of tourist foot traffic.  These guys could be making money hand over fist if they opened reliably.  (I imagine they could make an absolute killing if they opened for late-night, like the former pita place in this location did.)

There's no question that the tacos are excellent, but I fear for the future of Tacos El Rey if they can't maintain consistent opening hours.  If you happen to pass by and see that they're open, definitely go for it, but if you're deciding whether or not to make a trip, it's risky because they may not be open when you arrive.'}, {'review_stars': 5.0, 'review_date': '2015-11-20 22:10:41', 'review_text': 'Tacos de Barbacoa are at 100% on point. 
Taste just like the ones back in my Mexican hometown. 
Definitely recommend them.'}]}

The service was excellent and struck up a conversation with our waitress that also previously lived in the SF bay area. The food was excellent and pricing was so affordable. The lamb shank was near fall off the bone, and very tender and flavorful. Will return!"}, {'review_stars': 4.0, 'review_date': '2021-12-16 23:18:54', 'review_text': "Such an adorable place, it reminds me of my grandmother's house with all the floral patterns, the decor is for sure very welcoming. 

We went there for a friend's birthday brunch. We were driving from LA to Santa Barbara for a day trip and were running late so we called them on our way and told them we would be 15 minutes late and they were very accommodating.

As soon as we arrived, we were welcomed by the host and taken to our table outside, it was a little cold so we asked if we could move inside and they had one table available which they set for us.

They brought us warm sourdough bread and freshly baked mini blueberry muffins, the muffins were delicious. 

I ordered the CROQUE MADAME which comes with a sunny-side-up egg, parts of the egg white were not cooked and I had to separate them, I am pretty sure I have seen it in many culinary shows that you should not serve the eggs with the white still raw. The rest of the sandwich was good. I was going to take a star away for this but we had such a pleasant experience that I decided not to.

first, we spent around 4.5 hours there and they still didn't try to throw us out! Second, the waiter was very sweet, he brought my friend a dessert with a little candle at the end that we appreciated a lot.

All in all, it was a pleasant experience and I will go back again but probably won't order the same food."}, {'review_stars': 4.0, 'review_date': '2021-12-16 21:56:01', 'review_text': "My father-in-law and his fiancée both had birthdays in November, so Matt B. and I took them out to dinner at Stella Mare's the night before Thanksgiving. It was a cold and dark out, even for our 6:15 reservation, and the restaurant was lovely, with a warm, cozily upscale atmosphere. We ate in a greenhouse room with high ceilings, tons of windows, and a fireplace. Service was good, and we enjoyed a pleasant meal. Even our 20-month-old had a swell time, though at some point, he insisted on walking around and hanging out by the fireplace. (He was mercifully well behaved, but did make eyes at the other diners.)

We started with a round of cocktails, which were pretty solid. I had a Smoky Margarita with smoky chili-infused tequila, house sweet and sour, and agave, finished with a spicy rim and a grilled lime wheel. Matt had L'Ancien, with Remy Martin cognac, Bulleit rye whiskey, sweet vermouth, Benedictine, and Angostura and Peychaud's bitters. We switched to wine for the rest of our meal. I forget what exactly, but some kind of nice red, no complaints there.

The food was classic hearty bistro fare without much trendy flourish, ideal for dinner with grandparents, plus there was a kids' menu for our toddler--we ordered him pasta with butter and parmesan, but I think he mostly ate bread from the complimentary basket. The rest of us started with the cheese plate and deviled eggs. The cheese plate came with three unidentified but tasty cheeses, accompanied by crostini, strawberry slices, golden raisins, and some kind of fruit jam. The deviled eggs, or oeufs mimosa au homard, were quite good, supple and topped with lobster tail slices, tobiko, pickled shallots, and dill, perched on a slab of toasted French baguette over a bed of arugula and olive oil.

For our entrées, Matt and I split the canard a l'orange and the filet mignon. The duck breast was cooked a touch more than we like, but it was otherwise decent, served with parsnip purée, roasted broccoli florets, charred red wine-braised shallots, orange gastrique, candied orange zest, and pea shoots. The steak was tender and satisfying. We got it with the cognac peppercorn sauce and it came with a heap of thin fries.

Since we were celebrating, we ordered dessert. I got the lemon cake, which was light and bright, with lemon curd filling, caramelized mascarpone cream, and vanilla sauce. I also tried a bit of my father-in-law's crêpe Suzette, which was great, with housemade crêpes, Grand Marnier, orange reduction, orange segments, and powdered sugar. Matt got some McConnell's vanilla bean ice cream, which he shared with our son--baby's first hesitant taste of ice cream.

I liked Stella Mare's and would be happy to come back. It feels like a place that will be around forever, and I look forward to more family dinners in that elegant greenhouse."}]}

We got a table on the patio outside Lucky's but it took quite a long time to get served and there were further delays later (and discrepancies in service, compared to neighboring tables). The guy who brought the food seemed angry about something the way he barked out the dish names, asking which dish was for whom. Our main server was friendly though, which made up for his tardiness :-)

The veggie burger was good, although the blue cheese was a little too salty. I had high expectations for Herbie's potato skins (as I love potato skins) but they were a bit disappointing. However, the Japanese sautéed tofu dish was very good and this helped elevate the experience to memorable.

Chef Leonard Schwartz describes the dish as follows: "Composed of fresh baby spinach sautéed lightly in extra virgin olive oil and topped with slices of firm marinated tofu and wok-cooked shitake mushrooms and green onions, surrounded in a Japanese vinaigrette flavored with sesame oil, ginger, garlic, brown sugar, and lime juice."

We had a guest request a vegan dish about a year and a half ago, and that's when I put together the tofu dish," he said. Well, I'm glad you had one guest who cares about the planet! (And props to the chef too.)

I'm giving Lucky's only 2 Green Stars for social and environmental impact, for these simple reasons:
· Lucky's menu is meat-focused. It would be great if Lucky's made a stand and adapted its menu a little to help out with our climate crisis, not to mention mitigating deforestation and putting an end animal cruelty and the insidious negative impact that the meat industry has on society. You did a great job with the tofu and spinach dish, so keep going!
· No information is provided on food sourcing. Where does it come from? Are the eggs pasture-raised? Is the tofu organic? Communication of these things actually matter, even for something vegan like tofu, which ranges from industrially-farmed GM soy that's sprayed with bee-killing neonics to one of the most sustainable products on the planet (more here: https://greenstarsproject.org/2019/06/13/sustainable-tofu-brands-soy-deforestation-ethics/)'}, {'review_stars': 2.0, 'review_date': '2022-01-09 01:56:42', 'review_text': "Interestingly we've been here a few times, normally one of our stops for dinner while in town, however, this time was absolutely not great. This will more than likely be my last visit to Luckys, there are just too many restaurants that are doing good things to pay supremely overpriced fares for lackluster food"}, {'review_stars': 1.0, 'review_date': '2021-12-27 00:53:17', 'review_text': "Honestly the food is not worth the price you're paying. Staff could have been more lively but it seemed like they didn't care about us because we don't look of money. Now the food, the waiter recommended the $70 dollar steak which I said I would give a try if he's speaking so highly of it. I ordered it to be cooked medium. I got it rare, not even medium rare, but RARE. Next time it came out it was cooked a little too well but I didn't want to bother again so I tried it and my gosh was it bland. It tasted nothing like what was described and I don't even think there was either salt or pepper at the minimum. Not to mention the price is JUST for steak no sides or anything. So I ordered mashed potatoes as well and it tasted like BOX mashed potatoes. After that I lost my appetite and asked for the bill. Still ended up paying 100 for 1 entré and an appetizer. The Catch-22 was balanced and boozy, with Hennessy VSOP, Whistle Pig 10 yr, Dolin Rouge, and Benedictine.

We noticed a smoking cloche behind the bar and talked to the bartender about what it was used for, apparently a cocktail that wasn't on the current menu (but which he seemed perfectly able and ready to make). We had used Social Cycle for a friend's birthday party. I recall the pricing per person being fairly reasonable, and well-justified for the overall experience. The cycle travels through the Funk Zone and is quite the attraction for nearby pedestrians and restaurant-goers.

We had one lady in our party who would occasionally take breaks and the cycle would noticeably slow down. Pedal, pedal, pedal!

There was also some storage for our personal items. 

The only downside was that it felt a little unorganized, as in the places we stopped didn't know that our large group was planning to stop there. We were also told we would get discounts at our stops but we did not receive any discounts.

The first thing to understand about going to Billies during the post-bar period is it's one of a select few restaurants that are open until very early so it will literally be swamped with people, most of whom are too drunk to put three sensical words together.

The night I was there there had been a scheduling error and they were understaffed. With a line out the door there was only one person working the counter and one cook. Watching this man work his heart out and still have a positive attitude I gained a real respect for him. If I were in his place I'd be hanging myself in the back room.

We finally got to order and I was told by multiple people that the Mac and Cheese is iconic.

Food Tip: The Mac and Cheese is iconic.

It was priced decently and there are unlimited fountain drinks (which was fantastic) and while I was waiting for my order I geeked it up on the Star Trek pinball machine. Most of my friends were passed out in a booth and I was a light drinker that night so I had plenty of energy. 

Once the food came I discovered that portions are not something they care about at Billies. It was a massive bowl of Mac and Cheese for just a couple of bucks. I enjoyed the food do a long while, then shared it with some friends. There was plenty to go around.

By the time we left it had calmed down a little. I thanked the man behind the counter for being so nice and working so hard and told him I would recommend Billies to everyone I know. The nigiri is usually consistent, occasionally when it's take out they sometimes give you more rice but the fish size is always the same. Some nigiris at other sushi places are tiny and cost double the price but at Sushi Teri the price is decent in comparison to the size of their nigiri. Which is the main reason I come here. \nWe ordered quite a few things to try out other than our usual. We got the calamari, dynamite, four types of rolls, and uni, unagi and salmon nigiris. I really liked it for the fact that it's bigger with a lot of scallops and a few big mussels, the price itself was decent considering other places sell it for about the same price for the size of an appetizer. As for the calamari appetizer, it was a horrible fail, the batter was thin but it was not crispy, the initial bite felt like a dried jerky texture on the outside then after that initial bite you get the tenderness of the calamari. The uni nigiri was fresh as usual, the unagi and salmon was great! The four rolls we ordered were called Monkey roll, orange roll, broken heart roll and the real crab roll, we specifically got these roll because it didn't have any imitation crab on the inside. Even for cheap stuff such as vegetables or noodles that cost $6 and upwards, you would think you would get a decent amount.  No.  You also get a tiny tiny portion.

I ordered Orange Chicken for my son, and each piece of "chicken" was LITERALLY 50% potato starch by volume.  Yeah.  Soggy potato starch coating each tiny piece of chicken.  

With these portion sizes and prices, I feel like I am eating at a Japanese restaurant with slightly more flavor and less fake politeness.

Now let's talk about the other stuff.

The biggest thing that pissed me off is the "sauce and appetizer bar".  The hot pot menu is a huge list of items with small font size.  You check off what you want.  So after ordering the waiter (dressed sloppy in regular street clothes) said, "Please help yourself to some appetizers", which makes it sound like they are complimentary.  So I got  some mediocre appetizers.  Then when I get the bill I see I am charged $2 for each person for this, even though my wife and son didn't eat anything from there.

I asked the waiter, "What is this?  There is no sign about this?".  He states it's on the menu that I looked at for 10 seconds.  I was already upset at paying $124 for three people (we had to go eat somewhere else because we were still hungry), and I didn't feel like arguing with him.

The next thing that annoyed me was that before I was even done eating, the waiter hands me the check.

"Excuse me what does this say?", I asked, pointing to the Chinese characters.

"Oh, the noodles made from beans, right?"

"It's made from green beans, right?"
"Yes"
"Yeah, so they are made from beans"
He makes an annoying ugly stink face and shakes his head.

One of the saddest closings of a Santa Barbara restaurant throughout my culinary journey was Julienne. With the classiest establishment representing the combination of sophisticated French dishes with a twist of old school Americano. 

Late Owner/chef Justin West created an amazing menu, although I felt as if it didn't really have a distinct identity to it, it had a lot of fan favorites with a heavy focus on meat.

My boyfriend pointed me to this establishment when I first came to Santa Barbara saying it's very "date-worthy", "boundary-pushing haute cuisine", and also was one of his favorite restaurants for fine dining. I wanted to see if all the hype surrounding this place was worth it. I'm pleased to say Julienne had the BEST wine pairings with their prix fixe menu.

THE STAFF- 

To my surprise, Chef Justin was in the restaurant working the kitchen; not sure if he was expediting or what, but he came to our table after our meal and showed his gratitude for us coming in that night. It truly made that our stay feel special.

THE FOOD-

My boyfriend and I both ordered their five course tasting with wine pairing ($210 for both).

For starters we received a delicious Wild baby Arugula salad ($12) that was tousled in stone fruit with toasted oats, house ricotta, and onions with lemon oil. I was most impressed with how the lemon oil added a tart flavor to the stone fruit. 

After the starters, we received a delicious duck dish ($25 on menu) that had boneless confit, lentil risotto, bok choy, and plum sauce that was drizzled on the side. Although duck is definitely an acquired taste to most, the duck that we received was glistening and perfectly cooked, I'm sure anyone new to duck would fall in love!

We had the Roasted Ocean Trout ($26 on menu) which consisted mostly of lentils, artichoke, garlic, and dashi broth. The dashi broth and artichoke gave a very rich and aromatic flavor that was very palatable to my tastes.

We received the Fettuccine ($24 on menu), which consisted of tomatoes, fresh pork sausage, basil and Parmesan cheese. It must have been in season for the tomatoes because they were juicy and delicious. I was extremely impressed with the refined flavor of the dish which was very savory and didn't come out too heavy as one would normally expect from the typical fine dining French restaurant.

The Bone Marrow (not on menu) was my boyfriend's favorite part of the meal. I'm not even a huge fan of Bone Marrow, but the one I had at Julienne just had immaculate texture that gave me joy in every bite, it was absolutely phenomenal.

To finish off our meal, my boyfriend and I chose separate dishes. I had the Foie Gras & Donuts ($18 on menu) which had Torchon of foie, sourdough donuts, and strawberry balsamic preserves. My boyfriend had their sherbet I believe, and I know he liked it.

When he explained the tasting menu he said one of the three courses has to be a salad or appetizer, which some of the reviews seem to say otherwise but that's fine. We each chose an appetizer and two entrees.Took about 20 minutes for our appetizers to arrive. The mussels were excellent and the foie gras with strawberry jam was nice but not a stand out.  As these were light dishes, we were looking forward to our next course.  We continued to look forward for a very very long time. It was about 50 minutes until our next course came. Our third course was duck breast and beef cheeks.  Both were nicely prepared. So when i was invited to a friends bd dinner at the Tee Off, i jumped at the chance.
I/We always start with drinks and Schrooms though i think that the Tee has changed the presentation of these. But: the schroomers are still my favorite among all of the sauteed mushrooms i have eaten all over the world. Yes right here in Loud family SB!
Service wise: the Tee Off has always been dependent on whether the very experienced wait staff is having a good night or not. Yet the service has always been professional if it has not always been friendly or personal.
I think that this is the major issue about the Tee Off and has always been.
The Ladies of the Tee Off tend to as a general rule segregate their tables into categories: the tourists, the veterans, the others.
I have gotten great, personal service here and ok efficient service from a couple of servers.
But: i am smart enough to ignore any bs and just enjoy the company that i am with as well as the good to great food they serve here.
We started at the Bar and i have to say that the bartenders (Matt) here have always been friendly, enthusiastic and personal. Martinis and garlic bread? Yes!
We started with the famous Tee Off's plate of sautéed Mushrooms and they are delicious as always: chocked full of big fat small creminis', wine, veal demi-glace . Always delicious and they never change.
We all ordered Prime Rib and shared the large cut, med rare. It arrived exactly as ordered with a big fat Baked Potato plus sour dough rolls. We both had a salad with Blue cheese. The greens are crisp and cold and the dressing is jammed packed with real blue cheese chunks. The Tee Off does not skimp on the accoutrements or the primary entrees, appetizers or desserts as well as the drinks. Oh, the drinks! Heavy pours are the going thing here and have always been as far as i know.
The food here is and has always been first rate: prime for the Beef and top choice for the pork and chicken. This is food that you can write home about.
The Chef in charge has his foot and hand on all that goes out to the customer and the final products show this firm grip.
The Tee Off bar opens at 4 with the restaurant at 5 most days.
We arrived early, had drinks and then the food at a booth in front. Though for me either dining room is a good place to people watch.
You can make reservations if you like.
We had a primo time here and the server was friendly and pleasant and more to the point, efficient.

Along with their parents, the girls relocated to Canada as refugees where during their teens they discovered a passion for music. Heading south to LA by way of Atlanta, the singing sisters with a bold flair for fashion are now embarking on a musical journey, gearing up to unleash their Afro-pop sound to the world. CNN's African Voices caught up with the sister act -- known collectively as Faarrow (combining the translation of their names into English -- Iman means "Faith" and Siham means "Arrow") to talk about music, aspirations and Somalia. CNN: Hi guys, thanks for chatting with me today. What are some of your musical influences? Iman: We love Michael Jackson, Lionel Richie -- stuff my mom would listen to and play -- and the Spice Girls. We used to die for the Spice Girls. CNN: You are working on your debut album now -- how's that been? Siham: We've been working with Elijah Kelley -- he's actually an actor. He was in "Hairspray," "The Butler," and most recently he was in the George Lucas animation, "Strange Magic." That's what he's more known for but his first passion is music. He's an incredible producer, writer and singer. I just felt like he was always the missing piece. He brought everything together. CNN: So now that you've found your "missing piece," how would you describe your sound? Siham: Our music before was experimenting with Afrobeat sounds but now it's more of a fusion (of what) we are inspired by. It's pop with undertones of hip hop and rhythmic African percussion. It's a fusion of everything. CNN: And do you guys write the songs as well? Siham: The entire album was pretty much (written and produced) by me, my sister and Elijah. And when we signed we already had a lot of those songs already done. Warner Brothers Records is really great in that way that they already loved what we were doing and let us do our own thing. CNN: What are you listening to right now? Siham: Oh my God, there's so many! Iman: Sia with "Chandelier." Siham: I really love this new song -- I don't know if Iman is going to agree with me -- but his name's LunchMoney Lewis, it's called Bills; I love it. CNN: As well as your music, you both work with the U.N. High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) -- how did you start your humanitarian work? Iman: Ever since we were kids we wanted to help Somalia, we always talked about it. But we were like "what can we physically do?" We were doing some research and we called our mom and she said 'You know you still have family over there. There's a refugee camp in Kenya and your great uncle and his kids live in a refugee camp.' And we did some research about Dadaab refugee camp, it's a massive camp that has taken in Sudanese refugees, Somali refugees, Rwandan refugees -- pretty much anywhere there was a conflict. Everybody fled to Dadaab. In the beginning (it was) pure advocacy talking about it on Twitter and Facebook. CNN: But then you decided to "up your game" as it were... Iman: Yes, then we started a non-profit and we'd do small benefit concerts in Toronto and in San Diego -- wherever there was a big Somali community we would do outreach but all we had was our singing, working with UNHCR in a capacity as a spokesperson. We headlined World Refugee Day at the Kennedy Center, as well as the Nansen Awards twice in Geneva. We felt like this platform of singing -- the bigger it gets, the more we can do. Siham: We obviously love fashion so we wanted to do our own socially conscious brand so we've been making these bracelets and necklaces called "Wish Creatively." Wish stands for "Women Internationally Selling Hope." We wanted to do a socially conscious brand where we sell these bracelets where it goes back to projects in Kenya or Somalia with women providing them with a sustainable income. CNN: So what's next for you two? Siham: We're actually in the mixing process right now. We still have a few (tracks) to finish up but the majority of the album is pretty much done. We want to turn it in as soon as possible so they can put together a rollout plan and get ready for the first single to drop. Iman: I don't feel like we ever lost that feeling like we're creative spokespersons for our generation as well as for Somalia. I feel like now because we followed our dreams it's like 'they're not just refugees anymore.' We don't have to become doctors so we can one day give back to Somalia and help rebuild -- it's such a beautiful dream but not ours. In our culture, anything creative is not really respected or appreciated. But I feel like now but even with our new deal we're still trucking along. I feel like we inspire people. Woolfenden checked Leo for other injuries and thought, "Let's get out of here." That was before he noticed his Achilles tendon, which resembled transparent tape covered in blood, and his left tibia protruding from his boot. The boot was next to his left stump, he testified before a federal jury Thursday, the third day in which survivors and family members of those killed in the Boston Marathon bombing shared their stories -- often gruesome and heartbreaking -- in the sentencing phase for Dzhokhar Tsarnaev. The jury must decide whether the 21-year-old, who has been found guilty of perpetrating the attack with his now-deceased brother, will die for his crimes or spend his life in prison. Woolfenden, a biomedical researcher for the Novartis Institutes, recalled using his belt as a tourniquet on his leg and trying to comfort Leo. The boy cried, "Mommy! Daddy! Mommy! Daddy! Mommy! Daddy! Mommy! Daddy!" he testified. Amid the stench of "burning hair, blood, sulfur," a good Samaritan emerged. He gave Woolfenden another tourniquet and rushed Leo to safety. "I was completely terrified because I didn't know if I was ever going to see my son again. There was blood all over the sidewalk, all around me," he said. Dr. David King, a trauma surgeon at Massachusetts General Hospital, spent 14 years in the U.S. Army as a combat surgeon and served in Iraq and Afghanistan, as well as in Haiti after the devastating 2010 earthquake. "I arrived just as the first wave of casualties had shown up," he said. Heather Abbott had no such experience, so when one of the two bombs exploded, sending her through the doors of a restaurant and into a "puddle of chaos and blood and glass," her instinct was to run. She chose the latter. "It was probably the hardest decision I've had to make," she testified. Another marathon attendee rendered an amputee by the Tsarnaev brothers' attack, Marc Fucarile, recounted the two bombs going off. And though his left leg survived, it was severely burned, his calf muscle was blown off and his heel was shattered, he said. He hopes to save the left leg, he testified, but it's likely that it will have to be amputated above the knee. He takes more than 70 pills -- 24 pills in morning, 22 in afternoon, 26 at night -- to cope with his injuries, he said. One of the most dramatic points in Thursday's testimony involved the death of young Martin Richard. The prosecution showed close-up video of the 8-year-old, who was only 3½ feet from one of the bombs, according to the FBI's re-creation of the crime scene. His parents, Bill and Denise, are opposed to Tsarnaev receiving the death penalty and did not participate in the penalty phase, though Bill Richard did testify during the guilt phase. Dr. King told the court that Martin was especially vulnerable to the blast because he was so small and close to the ground, meaning the shrapnel more easily reached his head and torso. It's highly unlikely the boy died instantly, King said. Explaining that Martin died of rapid blood loss, King testified, "Receptors are generally not responsive to cutting. If you happen to be awake and someone cuts your bowel or liver, it generally does not hurt. What hurts is the stretching and twisting. ... Intestines were pulled and twisted; that would have caused visceral type pain." Woolfenden, the biomedical researcher, recalled seeing Martin and his mother shortly after the good Samaritan took Woolfenden's son, Leo, to safety. "I saw Martin's face, and I could see a boy that looked like he was fatally injured," he said,. Martin's hair was singed, his eyes had rolled back into his head and his mouth was agape. As for Martin's torso, "I saw an immense amount of blood. I was really, really terrified," Woolfenden said. He recalled Denise Richard pleading with her son, saying, "please" and "Martin" over and over. Woolfenden placed his hand on Denise Richard's back, he said. She turned to ask Woolfenden if he was OK. He said he was, and she turned her attention back to Martin. But no response came. According to testimony, Martin's aorta was nearly severed and he was eviscerated by shrapnel from the blast. "You'd have air quality alerts, and people who had respiratory problems or were vulnerable had to stay inside. We took action, and the air's a lot better." "There are a whole host of public health impacts that are going to hit home, so we've got to do better in protecting vulnerable Americans," Obama continued. "This is not just a future threat -- this is a present threat," said Brian Deese, a senior adviser to the President. "The good news is that, in addition to having doctors and nurses, public health officials, schools of medicine joining together to raise awareness -- and to in some cases impact their practice -- they anticipate, for example, increased asthma instances, and plan ahead of time to deal with those," Obama told Gupta. "What we have is companies like Google and Microsoft that are going to take data we're releasing and start developing apps so that, potentially, individual families are going to be able to monitor the air quality in their communities in a real-time basis." "Communities can start planning for prevention and mitigation efforts more effectively, and hopefully the other thing that happens is that families and parents join with these doctors and nurses to start putting some pressure on elected officials to try to make something happen to reduce the impacts of climate change," said Obama. The impacts of climate change on health will depend on a multitude of factors, according to the Environmental Protection Agency. "These factors include the effectiveness of a community's public health and safety systems to address or prepare for the risk and the behavior, age, gender, and economic status of individuals affected," the EPA says on its website. "Around the world, variations in climate are affecting, in profoundly diverse ways, the air we breathe, the food we eat, and the water we drink," writes Dr. Maria Neira, director of the WHO's public health and environment department. "We are losing our capacity to sustain human life in good health." "Consider air pollution, the single greatest environmental health risk we face. Floods are increasing in frequency and intensity, creating breeding grounds for disease-carrying insects. Mosquito-borne diseases, like malaria, are particularly sensitive to heat and humidity. What will happen if rising temperatures accelerate the lifecycle of the malaria parasite?" "Children and the elderly will be among the most vulnerable," writes Neira. "Areas with health infrastructure will be least able to cope. Developing countries will be hardest hit. The health gaps we have been trying hard to close may grow even wider." Earth's average temperature has risen by 1.4 degrees Fahrenheit over the past century, and is projected to rise an additional 2 degrees over the next hundred years, according to the EPA. "Small changes in the average temperature of the planet can translate to large and potentially dangerous shifts in climate and weather," the agency warns. Still, there exists a sizable group of people who doubt climate change is happening. A 2013 TIME magazine article makes the case that medical professionals may be the best messengers for global warming. "Framing global warming as a public health issue rather than as an environmental or national security one produces the most emotionally compelling response among people, since it focuses on the immediate implications a warmer climate would have on people's lives," the article says. "This strategy also has the benefit of providing a sense of hope that the problems can be addressed and avoided, if action is taken early enough." The President said what happened with Los Angeles' air proved that point. "When the Clean Air Act was passed, not only was there a terrible smog in Los Angeles, it was true in most metropolitan areas across the country," Obama said. "The fact is that air quality has dramatically improved and it's been much cheaper than anybody expected, because technology advanced and people figured out how to do it. As a consequence, the American people are a lot healthier, in addition to being able to, you know, see the mountains in the background because it's not covered in smog." "We know how to do this," Obama said. "We just have to be bold and recognize and trust the kind of innovative spirit that the American people have always displayed."

New York (CNN)A New York Police Department detective apologized Friday for an angry exchange with an Uber driver that was caught on video and landed him on modified assignment. "I apologize. I sincerely apologize," Patrick Cherry told WNBC's Jonathan Dienst. Cherry, an NYPD detective assigned to the FBI's Joint Terrorism Task Force with top-secret security clearance, faces suspension, reassignment or loss of his clearance after the video of the altercation went viral. "I was just angry, at that moment, for that exchange." Cherry said. "If the conversation initially went differently, we probably would have shook hands at the end of that encounter." The altercation began Monday when the Uber driver gestured to a detective in an unmarked car to use his blinker after he was allegedly attempting to park without using it, according to Sanjay Seth, a passenger in the car who uploaded the video to YouTube. The video picks up seconds after the detective began yelling at the driver and mocking his accent, and also shows the unmarked car with lights flashing pulled over behind the Uber car. In the video, the detective tells the driver he has committed "three traffic and law violations"  and then becomes irate and uses expletives toward the comparably calm driver. A visibly upset Police Commissioner William Bratton on Wednesday said the detective was placed on modified assignment pending an investigation. "No good cop can watch that without a wince," he told reporters. "As all good cops know ... the officer made their jobs a little bit harder. That kind of anger like that is unacceptable in any encounter; discourtesy like that and language like that is unacceptable. That officer's behavior reflected poorly on everyone who wears our uniform." Cherry also apologized to the police commissioner, saying "he's doing a lot of good work to strengthen community relations in the city and I set that back. I would work, personally work to fix that." "You can't judge three minutes of a tape involving me and me alone as indication that that's the kind of practice of the New York City Police Department," he added. Bratton, who said the detective was on duty at the time, issued an apology to the driver and passengers. Mayor Bill de Blasio told reporters at an unrelated news conference that he had not seen the video. "There's just no place for any public servant to use discriminatory or negative language," he said. "Obviously, our police play a particularly sensitive role and need to show respect for all people." In a statement, Michael Palladino, president of the Detectives' Endowment Association, called the officer "a person of good character and an excellent detective" who "should not be judged by one isolated incident." "I am not trying to minimize the significance of what occurred," Palladino said. "I am simply pointing out that cops are just like everyone else. They have families, friends and other things going on in their lives, too, that may affect their behavior at times. There is no disputing that we are held to a higher standard, that is why this incident is so newsworthy." In the video, after the detective steps back to his car, the Uber driver, who moved to America less than two years ago, turns to the passenger and thanks him for recording the video. "That's crazy. That's really inappropriate," the passenger tells the driver. "Listen, it's not your fault. He shouldn't be slamming your car door, throwing things around. He doesn't have a right to open your door." When the detective comes back, he is irate and doesn't allow the driver to speak. "I don't care what you have to say, people are allowed to park their cars on the side of the street without your interference," the detective tells the driver. "I don't know where you're coming from, where you think it's appropriate in doing that. That's the way it works." "I've got news for you," the detective says, "the only reason you're not in handcuffs and going to jail and getting summons in the precinct is because I have things to do. That's the only reason that's not happening. Because this isn't important enough for me. You're not important enough. Don't ever do that again." The NYPD said the Internal Affairs Bureau is investigating the incident. "The behavior in the video is wrong and unacceptable and we appreciate the NYPD investigating the incident. We are in touch with our driver-partner who was subjected to this terrible experience and will continue to provide any support he needs," Uber said Tuesday. Seth met Wednesday with an investigator from the Civilian Complaint Review Board, according to board spokeswoman Linda Sachs. The investigator is attempting to interview the driver as well as the police officer. The findings will be presented to the board, which will make an official determination on whether misconduct was committed, Sachs said. The board will either refer the decision to the NYPD with a disciplinary recommendation, or decide to bring up the officer on administrative charges, with the decision presented to the police commissioner. We weren't in a huge rush, but we didn't have hours to dine, because I had dough proofing at home and needed to attend to it. Hubby walked in and checked in with waitress and came out saying we should take any seat we wanted and she'd be right out with menus. So, we sat next to the Xmas tree on the patio and waited... and waited... and waited.

We figured she couldn't see us so we moved right next to the front door, where she couldn't miss us. After waiting several minutes, I decided we would wait 5 more minutes and then find somewhere else for dinner. After 3 minutes, a 30-something brunette waitress came and dropped off menus. I let her know the table was wobbly and she said she'd bring a block for it (which she never did). Oddly, I detected a twinge of attitude from the waitress with that exchange... not sure why, but there was an element of nastiness in her tone. She also turned on the table-side heater, which was very effective, if somewhat atmosphere-killing, because of its very bright coils.

I pulled up Yelp to look at the pics of the entrees and we settled on what we wanted to order. After about 5 more minutes, the waitress returned and informed us that dinner would take longer here than other restaurants. I let her know I was proofing dough at home and if we ordered immediately, how long would it take. She replied that if we ordered faster, the food would come faster. (Duh! Crazy how that works!) Again, a twinge of nastiness.

Hubby and I heard the specials and I asked her to repeat the sides on one of the specials. Attitude was ramping up from her. I asked her what her favorite item on the menu was and she replied, "I can't say. You're the one who's going to eat it, not me! The chef wouldn't even tell you." 

I didn't dare ask another question, because she was in full beeeyatch mode. We both ordered off the regular menu. Then, she said it would be 20 minutes and we ordered wine. Before she left, I asked the waitress if she could please ask the chef not to hold the entree until we finished the appetizer... just to put it all in at once. Her response? "The chef doesn't need to be micromanaged like that." And that, my Yelp friends, was my limit to the abuse I was going to take from this witch of a waitress.

I smiled and said thank you, but we aren't going to stay. She said okay, opened the door, walked into the restaurant, and I could hear her behind me exclaiming with a groan as she entered, "Oh my Gaaaaawwwdd."

Good thing they're in a tourist zone, because Petit Valentien can have a fresh supply of customers the waitress can abuse. As for this local, I'd recommend one of our other many fine dining establishments and give this one a wide berth, if service is important to you.

Two last comments: First, we noticed the restaurant had a Help Wanted sign posted, looking for servers, which could hint that maybe the attitude starts at the top and they have a hard time keeping servers. Second, I noticed another one of the local Yelp Elite crew (and other reviewers) also commented on the rude and inattentive service here. Seems to be a trend and unfortunately, it was our unlucky turn to experience it tonight.'}, {'review_stars': 1.0, 'review_date': '2021-10-08 16:53:58', 'review_text': "Just don't go. They are severely understaffed and incredibly rude. We had to walk out after the waitress ignored us for about 15-20 minutes. We had to ask her three times to let us know what the wait time would be. Very unprofessional."}, {'review_stars': 5.0, 'review_date': '2021-10-02 22:38:26', 'review_text': 'Amazing experience with a variety of Ethiopian flavors giving it the right touch. A story in flavors you need to experience. Lovely location feels like your in another world.  
Great service.'}]}

Based on the provided passages, the treatment for an asset discarded as per the Income Tax Act is as follows:

1. If an asset is discarded by a business without selling it, the scrap value of the asset can be reduced from the block of assets in the previous year in which the asset was discarded, as per Section 43(6) of the Income Tax Act.
2. The block concept applies to all assets falling within the same class and subject to the same rate of depreciation, and any alterations to the value of the block must be made in accordance with the provisions of Chapter IV D of the Income Tax Act, 1961.
3. If the assessee is entitled to claim depreciation on the residual value of a discarded asset, they can do so even if the asset is not used for business or professional purposes in the current year or subsequent years, as per Section 50(1) of the Income Tax Act.

Therefore, the treatment for an asset discarded as per the Income Tax Act is to reduce the scrap value from the block of assets in the previous year, follow the block concept for calculating depreciation, and claim depreciation on the residual value of the discarded asset even if it is not used for business or professional purposes.
